title: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies
authors: Stephen L. Hauser, Anthony A. Amato

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.title: Incidence and Demographics of Guillain-Barré Syndrome
items.content: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute autoimmune polyradiculoneuropathy with a consistent annual incidence. It affects more males than females, predominantly in adults over children.
keyPoints: Incidence: 10-20 cases per million annually
~5000–6000 cases occur per year in the United States
Males are at slightly higher risk than females
Adults are more frequently affected than children
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Guillain-Barré Syndrome
items.content: GBS presents with rapidly evolving areflexic motor paralysis, often ascending in nature. It can include sensory disturbances and autonomic dysfunction, requiring close monitoring.
keyPoints: Ascending paralysis is common, starting as rubbery legs
Facial diparesis present in 50% of patients
Bulbar weakness occurs in ~50% of cases
30% may require ventilatory assistance
Deep tendon reflexes attenuate or disappear within days
Autonomic involvement includes blood pressure fluctuations and cardiac dysrhythmias
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.title: Subtypes and Antecedent Events of Guillain-Barré Syndrome
items.content: GBS has several subtypes, with AIDP being the most common. It often follows an antecedent infection, notably Campylobacter jejuni.
keyPoints: Most common variant: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (AIDP)
Axonal variants include AMAN and AMSAN
Miller Fisher syndrome accounts for ~5% of cases
70% occur 1–3 weeks after an acute infection
20-30% preceded by Campylobacter jejuni or human herpes virus infections
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: Vaccine-Associated Guillain-Barre Syndrome Risk
items.content: Recent immunizations have been implicated in GBS, with varying risks associated with different vaccines. The swine influenza vaccine from 1976 had a notable association, while seasonal influenza vaccines show a risk of <1 per million. SARS-CoV-2 adenovirus vector vaccines pose an increased risk compared to mRNA vaccines.
keyPoints: Swine influenza vaccine (1976) notably associated with GBS
Seasonal influenza vaccines: GBS risk <1 per million
SARS-CoV-2 adenovirus vector vaccines have increased GBS risk, unlike mRNA vaccines
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.title: Autoimmune Basis and Pathogenesis of AIDP
items.content: AIDP, the most common type of GBS, has an autoimmune basis involving both cellular and humoral immune mechanisms. Evidence includes autoreactive T cells against myelin proteins and autoantibodies causing complement-dependent demyelination.
keyPoints: Autoimmune basis for AIDP
Cellular and humoral mechanisms contribute to tissue damage
Autoreactive T cells identified in peripheral blood, CSF, and nerves
Complement-dependent demyelination demonstrated
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.title: Molecular Mimicry and Gangliosides in GBS
items.content: GBS is thought to result from immune responses to nonself antigens that cross-react with host nerve tissue via molecular mimicry. Gangliosides, particularly GM1, are common targets, often following C. jejuni infection.
keyPoints: Molecular mimicry mechanism implicated in GBS
Gangliosides, especially GM1, targeted in 20–50% of cases
C. jejuni infection precedes anti-ganglioside antibodies in many cases
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.title: Pathophysiology of Demyelinating vs. Axonal Forms of GBS
items.content: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome, the pathophysiological mechanisms differ between demyelinating forms like AIDP and axonal forms such as AMAN/AMSAN. In AIDP, flaccid paralysis results from conduction block due to complement deposition on Schwann cells leading to myelin sheath damage, but axons remain intact allowing for potential rapid recovery through remyelination. Conversely, in AMAN and AMSAN, the primary issue is axonal degeneration or reversible conduction block caused by antibodies binding to ion channels at nodes of Ranvier, which can lead to slower recovery if severe axonal degeneration occurs.
keyPoints: Conduction block in AIDP due to complement deposition on Schwann cells
Rapid recovery possible in AIDP through remyelination
AMAN/AMSAN involve primary axonal damage or reversible conduction block
Severe axonal degeneration in AMAN/AMSAN leads to slower recovery
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.title: CSF and Electrophysiological Features of GBS
items.content: The diagnosis of Guillain-Barré Syndrome is supported by distinctive cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) findings, which include elevated protein levels (1–10 g/L [100–1000 mg/dL]) without pleocytosis. These changes typically appear after 48 hours from symptom onset and can be accompanied occasionally by a transient increase in white cell count. Electrophysiological studies reveal prolonged F-wave latencies, reduced CMAP amplitudes in AIDP, and low-amplitude CMAPs in AMAN/AMSAN due to conduction block or axonal degeneration.
keyPoints: CSF protein elevation (1–10 g/L) without pleocytosis
Transient CSF white cell count increase possible
AIDP: prolonged F-wave latencies, reduced CMAP amplitudes
AMAN/AMSAN: low-amplitude CMAPs due to conduction block or axonal degeneration
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Differential Diagnosis of GBS
items.content: Guillain-Barré Syndrome is diagnosed based on clinical criteria including rapidly evolving paralysis with areflexia, absence of systemic symptoms, and characteristic antecedent events. The Brighton Collaboration's case definitions aid in epidemiologic studies and risk assessments related to vaccination. Differential diagnoses include conditions like acute myelopathies, diphtheria, tick-borne paralyses, vasculitic neuropathy, poliomyelitis, CMV polyradiculitis, critical illness neuropathy/myopathy, neuromuscular junction disorders, poisonings, and severe hypophosphatemia.
keyPoints: Diagnosis based on rapidly evolving paralysis with areflexia
Brighton Collaboration case definitions for epidemiologic studies

Differential diagnoses include acute myelopathies, diphtheria, tick-borne paralyses, vasculitic neuropathy, poliomyelitis, CMV polyradiculitis, critical illness neuropathy/myopathy, neuromuscular junction disorders, poisonings, severe hypophosphatemia
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.title: Initiating Treatment for GBS: Timing and Modalities
items.content: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS), treatment should begin as soon as possible after diagnosis. The effectiveness of immunotherapy diminishes approximately two weeks after the onset of motor symptoms, making early intervention crucial.
keyPoints: Treatment with IVIg or PLEX should be initiated promptly; each day counts.
~2 weeks after first motor symptoms, immunotherapy may no longer be effective.
IVIg is often preferred due to ease and safety, administered as 5 daily infusions totaling 2 g/kg body weight.
PLEX involves ~40–50 mL/kg plasma exchange 4–6 times over 7–12 days.
Meta-analysis shows treatment reduces need for mechanical ventilation from 27% to 14%, increases full recovery at 1 year from 55% to 68%.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.title: Monitoring and Management in the Worsening Phase of GBS
items.content: During the worsening phase of GBS, patients require critical care monitoring with attention to vital capacity, heart rhythm, blood pressure, and other factors.
keyPoints: ~30% of GBS patients need ventilatory assistance, sometimes for weeks or longer.
Monitoring includes vital capacity, heart rhythm, blood pressure, nutrition, DVT prophylaxis, cardiovascular status.
Consider tracheotomy after 2 weeks of intubation and perform chest physiotherapy.
Frequent turning, skin care, range-of-motion exercises, and reassurance are essential for recovery.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.title: Prognosis and Recovery in GBS
items.content: The prognosis for GBS is generally favorable, with most patients achieving full recovery within months to a year. However, factors such as severe axonal damage can worsen outcomes.
keyPoints: Approximately 85% of GBS patients achieve full functional recovery in several months to a year.
Mortality rate is <5% in optimal settings; death usually from secondary pulmonary complications.
Severe proximal motor and sensory axonal damage (522) worsens prognosis.
Advanced age, fulminant/severe attack, delayed treatment onset are negative prognostic factors.
Elevated serum neurofilament light chains and high anti-GM1 antibodies correlate with poor recovery.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.title: Distinguishing CIDP from GBS: Chronic Course and Variants
items.content: CIDP is differentiated from GBS by its chronic progression. It shares features with GBS, such as elevated CSF protein levels and demyelination on EDx. CIDP can be triggered by immune checkpoint inhibitors. Clinical manifestations include gradual onset over months, motor and sensory symptoms, and possible cranial nerve involvement. Variants like MADSAM (Lewis-Sumner syndrome) show asymmetric limb weakness.
keyPoints: CIDP has a chronic course unlike GBS's acute presentation.
Elevated CSF protein levels are common in CIDP, similar to GBS.
Variants include MADSAM with asymmetric limb weakness and CISP with sensory ataxia.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.title: Diagnosis of CIDP: Clinical, CSF, and Electrophysiologic Criteria
items.content: CIDP diagnosis relies on clinical presentation, CSF analysis showing elevated protein without pleocytosis, and EDx findings indicating demyelination. MRI or ultrasound can complement EDx by revealing nerve enlargement. It's crucial to exclude other conditions like vasculitis and diabetes mellitus.
keyPoints: CSF in CIDP is usually acellular with elevated protein levels.
EDx reveals conduction slowing, prolonged distal latencies, and conduction block.
MRI or ultrasound can show nerve enlargement, aiding diagnosis.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.title: Pathogenesis and Treatment Response in CIDP
items.content: CIDP is immune-mediated, responding to glucocorticoids unlike GBS. Biopsy shows onion-bulb changes from demyelination. A minority have serum antibodies against myelin proteins. Up to 25% of CIDP patients have MGUS, with IgA or IgG kappa cases responding well to treatment.
keyPoints: CIDP responds to glucocorticoids, indicating an immune-mediated pathogenesis.
Onion-bulb changes in biopsy suggest recurrent demyelination and remyelination.
25% of CIDP patients have MGUS; IgA or IgG kappa cases respond well to treatment.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.title: Initiating Treatment for CIDP: When and How?
items.content: Treatment for Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy (CIDP) is generally initiated when there is rapid progression or compromised walking ability. For mild cases, expectant management may be appropriate until spontaneous remission occurs.
keyPoints: Initiate treatment if progression is rapid or walking is compromised.
Mild cases can be managed expectantly awaiting spontaneous remission.
High-dose IVIg (2.0 g/kg body weight over 2–5 days) is the initial therapy, with three monthly courses recommended before concluding failure.
If response occurs, adjust infusion intervals or dosage (e.g., starting at 1 g/kg every 3–4 weeks).
One-third of patients may not respond adequately to initial therapy; consider alternative treatments.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy

items.title: Alternative Therapies and Considerations for CIDP Treatment Failure
items.content: For patients who do not respond to first-line therapies such as IVIg, scIg, PLEX, or glucocorticoids, alternative treatments like immunosuppressive agents (azathioprine, methotrexate, cyclosporine, cyclophosphamide) may be beneficial. Additionally, efgartigimod alfa has shown promise in preventing CIDP relapses.
keyPoints: Immunosuppressive agents can be used if initial therapies fail (azathioprine, methotrexate, cyclosporine, cyclophosphamide).
Efgartigimod alfa has demonstrated effectiveness in preventing CIDP relapses.
Periodic reassessment of risks and benefits is necessary when using immunosuppressive agents.
Consider evaluating for nodopathy, paranodopathy, or POEMS syndrome if treatment fails.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy

items.title: Differentiating Nodopathies/Paranodopathies from CIDP
items.content: Nodopathies and paranodopathies are distinct from CIDP due to the presence of autoantibodies targeting nodal and paranodal regions, specifically IgG4 antibodies against proteins like CNTN1 or NF155. These conditions manifest with progressive symmetric weakness, sensory ataxia, and postural tremor. Unlike typical CIDP, they often present with renal complications in cases involving CNTN1.
keyPoints: Approximately 10% of patients previously considered to have CIDP are reclassified as nodopathies or paranodopathies due to specific autoantibodies.
IgG4 antibodies against contactin-1 (CNTN1) and neurofascin 155 (NF155) are common, with CNTN1 also linked to renal issues like membranous glomerulonephritis.
These neuropathies show electrophysiological patterns similar to CIDP but are less responsive to IVIg; rituximab is a more effective treatment.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY

items.title: Identifying and Treating Multifocal Motor Neuropathy (MMN)
items.content: Multifocal motor neuropathy presents with progressive, asymmetric motor weakness and atrophy in selected nerve trunks, often affecting the arms more than legs. It is associated with persistent focal motor conduction block and can be confused with ALS. Less than 50% of patients have high titers of IgM antibodies to GM1 gangliosides.
keyPoints: MMN affects >75% male patients, presenting with progressive motor weakness and atrophy in specific nerve trunks.
Persistent focal motor conduction block is a key diagnostic feature; sensory fibers are relatively spared.
High-dose IVIg or scIg is effective for most patients, while rituximab or cyclophosphamide may benefit refractory cases. Glucocorticoids and plasmapheresis are not effective.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE
items.title: Distinguishing Features of Osteosclerotic Myeloma-Associated Neuropathy
items.content: Neuropathies associated with osteosclerotic myeloma differ significantly from those linked to lytic myeloma. They often present as demyelinating or mixed axonal and demyelinating neuropathies, resembling CIDP, and may respond to radiation therapy or surgical removal of the primary lesion.
keyPoints: Osteosclerotic myeloma accounts for ~3% of all cases but is linked with polyneuropathy in 50% of these cases.
Neuropathies are demyelinating/mixed, elevated CSF protein, and resemble CIDP clinically.
These neuropathies often respond to radiation therapy or removal of the primary lesion.
Associated monoclonal proteins typically involve lambda light chains.
POEMS syndrome may be present with systemic findings like skin thickening, hyperpigmentation, and increased VEGF levels.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE

items.subtopic: MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE
items.title: Characteristics of Anti-MAG Neuropathy in IgM-kappa MGUS
items.content: IgM-kappa monoclonal gammopathy can lead to a distinct sensory neuropathy, often with tremor and sensory ataxia. This condition is characterized by binding of the monoclonal IgM to MAG, leading to demyelination and remyelination, but it typically does not respond well to immunotherapy.
keyPoints: IgM-kappa MGUS associated neuropathy often presents with sensory symptoms, tremor, and ataxia.
Most patients are men over 50 years old.
Monoclonal IgM binds specifically to MAG in peripheral nerves, affecting myelin sheaths.
Demyelination and remyelination occur, but axonal loss develops over time.
Anti-MAG polyneuropathies are refractory to immunotherapy.
30% of MGUS cases may progress to multiple myeloma or lymphoma within 10 years.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE

items.title: Peripheral Nerve Involvement in Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN)
items.content: Polyarteritis nodosa frequently involves peripheral nerves, presenting as multifocal motor-sensory neuropathy due to ischemic lesions. This pattern is common clinically and confirmed postmortem.
keyPoints: Peripheral nerve involvement occurs in half of all PAN cases clinically and 100% at postmortem studies.
Most common presentation: multifocal (asymmetric) motor-sensory neuropathy (mononeuropathy multiplex).
Caused by ischemic lesions affecting small- to medium-sized arteries, particularly the epineural vessels of vasa nervorum.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · VASCULITIC NEUROPATHY

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Vasculitic Neuropathy
items.content: Suspected vasculitic neuropathy is diagnosed through nerve and muscle biopsy, often revealing systemic involvement. Treatment involves immunosuppressive agents like glucocorticoids and cyclophosphamide or rituximab.
keyPoints: Diagnosis: combined nerve and muscle biopsy using serial section or skip-serial techniques.
Approximately one-third of cases are 'nonsystemic,' affecting only peripheral nerves without constitutional symptoms.
Management includes glucocorticoids, cyclophosphamide, or rituximab; 5-year survival rates >80% with these treatments.
Rituximab plus glucocorticoids is recommended as standard initial treatment for ANCA-associated vasculitis.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · VASCULITIC NEUROPATHY

items.title: Association of Anti-Hu Paraneoplastic Neuroathy with Small-Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC)
items.content: Anti-Hu paraneoplastic neuroathy is often linked to small-cell lung cancer. Awareness of this association is crucial for timely diagnosis and management, as the neurological symptoms typically precede the identification of SCLC by up to 6 months.
keyPoints: More than half of subacute sensory neuronopathy cases are paraneoplastic, primarily related to lung cancer, especially small-cell lung cancer (SCLC).
Neurologic symptoms usually precede the identification of SCLC by ≤ 6 months.
Diagnosis involves testing for paraneoplastic antibodies and often PET scanning for tumor detection.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · ANTI-Hu PARANEOPLASTIC NEUROPATHY

items.title: Clinical Course and Treatment Challenges of Anti-Hu Paraneoplastic Neuroathy
items.content: The sensory neuronopathy associated with Anti-Hu paraneoplastic neuroathy progresses rapidly, leading to significant disability. It is typically unresponsive to standard treatments such as glucocorticoids, IVIg, PE, or immunosuppressant drugs.
keyPoints: Sensory neuronopathy runs its course in a few weeks or months and stabilizes, leaving the patient disabled.
Most cases are unresponsive to treatment with glucocorticoids, IVIg, PE, or immunosuppressant drugs.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · ANTI-Hu PARANEOPLASTIC NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.question: Which subtype of Guillain-Barré syndrome is most commonly associated with antibodies to the ganglioside GQ1b?
items.explanation: Miller Fisher syndrome is associated with antibodies to the ganglioside GQ1b.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.question: What percentage of Guillain-Barré syndrome cases are preceded by an infection with Campylobacter jejuni?
items.explanation: 20–30% of Guillain-Barré syndrome cases in North America, Europe, and Australia are preceded by Campylobacter jejuni infection.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.question: Which clinical feature is NOT typically associated with Guillain-Barré syndrome?
items.explanation: Fever and constitutional symptoms are absent at the onset of Guillain-Barré syndrome.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.question: Which variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome is characterized by rapidly evolving ataxia, areflexia without weakness, and ophthalmoplegia?
items.explanation: Miller Fisher syndrome presents with rapidly evolving ataxia, areflexia without weakness, and ophthalmoplegia.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.question: Which type of influenza vaccine has been associated with the highest risk of Guillain-Barre Syndrome per million persons vaccinated?
items.explanation: The swine influenza vaccine, administered widely in the United States in 1976, is noted for having a higher association with GBS cases.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.question: Which type of vaccines does not appear to carry an increased risk of Guillain-Barre Syndrome?
items.explanation: Meningococcal vaccinations (Menactra) do not appear to carry an increased risk of GBS.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is associated with the presence of anti-GQ1b antibodies in Guillain-Barre Syndrome?
items.explanation: Anti-GQ1b antibodies are associated with extraocular motor nerve involvement in GBS.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.question: What is the likely mechanism by which C. jejuni infection leads to Guillain-Barre Syndrome?
options: Direct bacterial invasion of nerve tissue
Antigenic cross-reactivity with gangliosides in human nerves
Activation of T cells against peripheral myelin proteins
Induction of complement-dependent demyelination by anti-GM1 antibodies
items.explanation: C. jejuni infection leads to GBS through antigenic cross-reactivity with gangliosides in human nerves.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) in Guillain-Barré Syndrome?
options: Elevated CSF protein level without pleocytosis
Increased CSF white cell count with pleocytosis
Normal CSF findings throughout the disease course
items.explanation: CSF in GBS typically shows an elevated protein level without accompanying pleocytosis.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.question: In Acute Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy (AIDP), what is the early electrophysiological feature?
options: Reduced amplitude of sensory nerve action potentials
Conduction block and reduced CMAP amplitudes
Slowing of conduction velocity without changes in latency
Increased F-wave latencies with normal distal latencies
items.explanation: Early electrophysiological features include prolonged F-wave latencies, prolonged distal latencies, and reduced amplitudes of CMAPs.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.question: What is the implication of low-amplitude compound muscle action potentials in AMAN?
options: Reversible conduction block due to antibody binding
items.explanation: Low-amplitude CMAPs in AMAN often result from reversible conduction block due to antibodies binding to ion channel proteins.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a differential diagnosis for Guillain-Barré Syndrome?
items.explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is not listed as a differential diagnosis for GBS.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following laboratory findings is NOT typically associated with Guillain-Barré Syndrome?
options: Elevated CSF protein level early in GBS
EDx features minimal early in GBS
items.explanation: The CSF protein level may not rise until the end of the first week.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.question: What is a recommended initial therapy for typical GBS due to its ease of administration and good safety record?
items.explanation: IVIg is often the initial therapy chosen because of its ease of administration and good safety record.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.question: Which treatment has not been found to be effective in GBS?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids have not been found to be effective in GBS.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.question: What percentage of patients with typical GBS achieve a full functional recovery within several months to a year?
items.explanation: Approximately 85% of patients with GBS achieve a full functional recovery.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.question: Which variant of CIDP is characterized by asymmetric weakness due to involvement of discrete peripheral nerves?
options: Multifocal acquired demyelinating sensory and motor (MADSAM) neuropathy
items.explanation: MADSAM neuropathy, also known as Lewis-Sumner syndrome, is characterized by asymmetric weakness due to involvement of discrete peripheral nerves.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.question: What is a certain sign of an acquired demyelinating process in electrophysiologic findings for CIDP?
items.explanation: The presence of conduction block is a certain sign of an acquired demyelinating process.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.question: Which condition should be considered if CSF pleocytosis is observed in CIDP?
items.explanation: CSF pleocytosis should lead to the consideration of HIV infection, leukemia or lymphoma, and neurosarcoidosis.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.question: Which imaging modality is often more readily available and can show enlargement of nerves in CIDP?
items.explanation: Ultrasound is cheaper and often more readily available for showing nerve enlargement.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.question: Which initial therapy is generally recommended for CIDP?
options: IVIg administered as 2.0 g/kg body weight over 2–5 days
PLEX initiated at 2–3 treatments per week
items.explanation: IVIg is usually the initial therapy for CIDP.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy

items.question: What are common long-term adverse effects of glucocorticoid treatment in CIDP?
options: Bone demineralization, gastrointestinal bleeding, and cushingoid changes
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids can cause bone demineralization, gastrointestinal bleeding, and cushingoid changes.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy

items.question: What should be considered if a patient with CIDP-like neuropathy fails to respond to treatment?
options: Evaluation for nodopathy, paranodopathy, or POEMS syndrome
Switching directly from IVIg to PLEX
items.explanation: Evaluation for nodopathy, paranodopathy, or POEMS syndrome is important if treatment fails.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy

items.subtopic: NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY
items.question: Which antibody is most commonly associated with nodopathies and paranodopathies?
items.explanation: Nodopathies and paranodopathies are associated with IgG4 isotype antibodies directed against nodal or paranodal antigens like CNTN1 or NF155.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY
items.question: Which treatment is less effective for nodopathies and paranodopathies due to their IgG4 antibody association?
items.explanation: Nodopathies and paranodopathies are less responsive to IVIg because they are usually associated with IgG4 antibodies.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY
items.question: Which condition is often confused with multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) due to similar presentations?
items.explanation: Some cases of multifocal motor neuropathy have been confused with lower motor neuron forms of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE
items.question: Which type of monoclonal protein is almost always associated with myeloma that has osteosclerotic features?
items.explanation: Myeloma with osteosclerotic features is associated almost always with lambda light chains.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE

items.subtopic: MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE
items.question: What syndrome is characterized by neuropathy, skin thickening, hyperpigmentation, and increased VEGF levels?
items.explanation: POEMS syndrome is characterized by neuropathy, skin thickening, hyperpigmentation, and increased VEGF levels.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE

items.subtopic: MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE
items.question: Which type of polyneuropathy is typically refractory to standard CIDP treatments in patients with osteosclerotic myeloma?
options: Demyelinating or mixed axonal and demyelinating neuropathies
items.explanation: Neuropathies in osteosclerotic myeloma are typically refractory to standard CIDP treatments and are demyelinating or mixed axonal and demyelinating.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE

items.question: Which pattern of neuropathy is most commonly associated with polyarteritis nodosa (PAN)?
items.explanation: The most common pattern in PAN is multifocal motor-sensory neuropathy due to ischemic lesions.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · VASCULITIC NEUROPATHY

items.question: What type of antibodies are associated with eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis?
items.explanation: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis is associated with PR3/cANCAs.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · VASCULITIC NEUROPATHY

items.question: Which treatment combination is recommended as the standard initial therapy for ANCA-associated vasculitis?
items.explanation: Combination therapy with glucocorticoids and rituximab is recommended as the standard initial treatment for ANCA-associated vasculitis.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · VASCULITIC NEUROPATHY

items.question: Which type of cancer is most commonly associated with anti-Hu paraneoplastic neurotoxicity?
items.explanation: More than half of the cases of anti-Hu paraneoplastic neurotoxicity are related to small-cell lung cancer.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · ANTI-Hu PARANEOPLASTIC NEUROPATHY

items.question: What is a frequent feature resulting from extensive deafferentation in anti-Hu paraneoplastic neurotoxicity?
items.explanation: Marked sensory ataxia is a frequent feature due to extensive deafferentation in anti-Hu paraneoplastic neurotoxicity.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · ANTI-Hu PARANEOPLASTIC NEUROPATHY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically responsive to treatment in anti-Hu paraneoplastic neurotoxicity?
items.explanation: Most cases are unresponsive to glucocorticoids, IVIg, PE, or immunosuppressant drugs; antibiotics are not typically used.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · ANTI-Hu PARANEOPLASTIC NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.statement: Deep tendon reflexes typically disappear within the first few days of Guillain-Barré syndrome onset.
items.explanation: In GBS, deep tendon reflexes attenuate or disappear within the first few days.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.statement: Fever is a common initial symptom of Guillain-Barré syndrome.
items.explanation: Fever and constitutional symptoms are absent at the onset of GBS.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.statement: The risk of Guillain-Barre Syndrome is increased with the use of messenger RNA vaccines for SARS-CoV-2.
items.explanation: There appears to be no increased risk of GBS with mRNA vaccines for SARS-CoV-2.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.statement: Anti-GM1 antibodies are common in all subtypes of Guillain-Barre Syndrome.
items.explanation: Antiganglioside antibodies, most frequently to GM1, are common particularly in AMAN and AMSAN.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.statement: In AIDP, complement deposition on Schwann cells leads to vesicular disintegration of the myelin sheath.
items.explanation: Complement deposition along Schwann cells initiates vesicular disintegration of the myelin in AIDP.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.statement: In AMAN, complement is deposited on sensory nerve roots.
items.explanation: In AMAN, antibodies against GD1a have specificity for motor rather than sensory nerve roots.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.statement: A combination of IVIg and plasmapheresis is significantly better than either treatment alone for typical GBS.
items.explanation: A combination of the two therapies is not significantly better than either alone.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.statement: Approximately 30% of patients with GBS require ventilatory assistance.
items.explanation: ~30% of patients with GBS require ventilatory assistance.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.statement: CIDP has a higher incidence than GBS.
items.explanation: The incidence of CIDP is lower than that of GBS.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.statement: Death from CIDP is common.
items.explanation: Death from CIDP is uncommon.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.statement: IVIg is administered as a single dose over one day.
items.explanation: IVIg is given in divided doses over 2–5 days.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy

items.statement: As many as one-third of patients with CIDP may fail to respond adequately to initial therapy.
items.explanation: A significant portion of patients may not respond well to the first treatment.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy

items.subtopic: NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY
items.statement: Multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) typically affects sensory fibers.
items.explanation: In MMN, sensory fibers are relatively spared.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY
items.statement: High-dose IVIg is an effective treatment for multifocal motor neuropathy.
items.explanation: Most patients with MMN respond to high-dose IVIg.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · NODOPATHIES AND PARANODOPATHIES | MULTIFOCAL MOTOR NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE
items.statement: Neuropathies associated with MGUS are usually responsive to immunotherapies designed to reduce monoclonal protein concentration.
items.explanation: Most neuropathies associated with MGUS do not respond to immunotherapies aimed at reducing monoclonal protein concentration.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE

items.subtopic: MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE
items.statement: Anti-MAG polyneuropathies are typically responsive to immunotherapy.
items.explanation: Anti-MAG polyneuropathies are refractory to immunotherapy.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · MULTIPLE MYELOMA | MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE

items.statement: Approximately one-third of biopsy-proven cases of vasculitic neuropathy are systemic, affecting multiple organs.
items.explanation: One-third of biopsy-proven cases are 'nonsystemic', affecting only peripheral nerves.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · VASCULITIC NEUROPATHY

items.statement: Constitutional symptoms are always present in vasculitic neuropathy.
items.explanation: In 'nonsystemic' vasculitic neuropathy, constitutional symptoms are absent.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · VASCULITIC NEUROPATHY

items.statement: Anti-Hu paraneoplastic neurotoxicity is primarily associated with non-small-cell lung cancer.
items.explanation: It is primarily related to small-cell lung cancer, not non-small-cell lung cancer.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · ANTI-Hu PARANEOPLASTIC NEUROPATHY

items.statement: Neurologic symptoms in anti-Hu paraneoplastic neurotoxicity often precede the identification of SCLC by up to 6 months.
items.explanation: Neurologic symptoms usually precede, by ≤ 6 months, the identification of small-cell lung cancer.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · ANTI-Hu PARANEOPLASTIC NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.assertion: Most patients with Guillain-Barré syndrome require hospitalization.
items.reason: The need for ventilatory assistance is associated with more severe weakness on admission.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why most patients require hospitalization.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.subtopic: Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)
items.assertion: Antecedent infections are rarely associated with Guillain-Barré syndrome.
items.reason: Approximately 70% of GBS cases occur after an acute infectious process.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; antecedent infections are commonly associated with GBS.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · Guillain-Barré Syndrome and Other Immune-Mediated Neuropathies | GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 1)

items.assertion: Guillain-Barre Syndrome can be triggered by older-type rabies vaccines.
items.reason: The mechanism is presumably immunization against neural antigens.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.assertion: The presence of anti-GQ1b antibodies is common in all forms of Guillain-Barre Syndrome.
items.reason: Extraocular motor nerves are enriched in GQ1b gangliosides compared to limb nerves.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; anti-GQ1b antibodies are not found in other forms of GBS unless there is extraocular motor nerve involvement.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 2)

items.assertion: Secondary axonal degeneration in severe cases of AIDP correlates with slower recovery.
items.reason: More secondary axonal degeneration results in a greater degree of residual disability.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.assertion: The primary electrophysiological finding in AMAN is conduction slowing.
items.reason: AMAN involves antibodies binding to ion channel proteins, causing reversible conduction block.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the primary finding is reduced amplitude of CMAPs due to conduction block.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 3)

items.assertion: Patients with severe proximal motor and sensory axonal damage have the worst prognosis.
items.reason: Successful regeneration cannot occur in cases of severe axonal damage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.assertion: Elevated serum levels of neurofilament light chains indicate a good recovery prognosis.
items.reason: High titers of serum anti-GM1 antibodies are associated with poor recovery in GBS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; elevated serum levels of neurofilament light chains are associated with more axonal involvement and poor recovery.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (part 4) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome

items.reason: The response to therapy suggests that CIDP is immune-mediated.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why CIDP responds to glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.assertion: All patients with CIDP have serum antibodies against P0 or myelin P2 protein.
items.reason: A minority of patients have serum antibodies against these proteins.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; only a minority of patients have these antibodies.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY

items.assertion: Anti-Hu paraneoplastic neurotoxicity involves an immune response characterized by antibodies and cytotoxic T cells.
items.reason: The target antigens are RNA-binding proteins expressed in both SCLC and dorsal root ganglion neurons, leading to cross-reactivity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the immune response occurs.
items.reference: Guillain Barr Syndrome and Other Immune Mediated Neuropathies · ANTI-Hu PARANEOPLASTIC NEUROPATHY
